name: Eigenwijs.Playground

unions.name: Color
unions.comment:
Represents a color.

The colors below, like `red` and `green`, come from the [Tango palette][tango].
It provides a bunch of aesthetically reasonable colors. Each color comes with a
light and dark version, so you always get a set like `lightYellow`, `yellow`,
and `darkYellow`.

[tango]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tango_Desktop_Project

unions.name: Game
unions.comment: Game model containing the visibility status, custom data (memory) and the Computer state record.

unions.name: Shape
unions.comment:
Shapes help you make a `picture`, `animation`, or `game`.

Read on to see examples of [`circle`](#circle), [`rectangle`](#rectangle),
[`words`](#words), [`image`](#image), and many more!

unions.name: Time
unions.comment:
The current time.

Helpful when making an [`animation`](#animation) with functions like
[`spin`](#spin), [`wave`](#wave), and [`zigzag`](#zigzag).

aliases.name: Computer
aliases.comment:
When writing a [`game`](#game), you can look up all sorts of information
about your computer:

  - [`Mouse`](#Mouse) - Where is the mouse right now?
  - [`Keyboard`](#Keyboard) - Are the arrow keys down?
  - [`Screen`](#Screen) - How wide is the screen?
  - [`Time`](#Time) - What time is it right now?

So you can use expressions like `computer.mouse.x` and `computer.keyboard.enter`
in games where you want some mouse or keyboard interaction.
aliases.type: { mouse : Eigenwijs.Playground.Mouse, keyboard : Eigenwijs.Playground.Keyboard, screen : Eigenwijs.Playground.Screen, time : Eigenwijs.Playground.Time }

aliases.name: Keyboard
aliases.comment:
Figure out what is going on with the keyboard.

If someone is pressing the UP and RIGHT arrows, you will see a value like this:

    { up = True
    , down = False
    , left = False
    , right = True
    , space = False
    , enter = False
    , shift = False
    , backspace = False
    , keys = Set.fromList [ "ArrowUp", "ArrowRight" ]
    }

So if you want to move a character based on arrows, you could write an update
like this:

    update computer y =
        if computer.keyboard.up then
            y + 1

        else
            y

Check out [`toX`](#toX) and [`toY`](#toY) which make this even easier!

**Note:** The `keys` set will be filled with the name of all keys which are
down right now. So you will see things like `"a"`, `"b"`, `"c"`, `"1"`, `"2"`,
`"Space"`, and `"Control"` in there. Check out [this list][list] to see the
names used for all the different special keys! From there, you can use
[`Set.member`][member] to check for whichever key you want. E.g.
`Set.member "Control" computer.keyboard.keys`.

[list]: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/KeyboardEvent/key/Key_Values
[member]: /packages/elm/core/latest/Set#member
aliases.type: { up : Basics.Bool, down : Basics.Bool, left : Basics.Bool, right : Basics.Bool, space : Basics.Bool, enter : Basics.Bool, shift : Basics.Bool, backspace : Basics.Bool, keys : Set.Set String.String }

aliases.name: Mouse
aliases.comment:
Figure out what is going on with the mouse.

You could draw a circle around the mouse with a program like this:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update 0

    view computer memory =
        [ circle yellow 40
            |> moveX computer.mouse.x
            |> moveY computer.mouse.y
        ]

    update computer memory =
        memory

You could also use `computer.mouse.down` to change the color of the circle
while the mouse button is down.
aliases.type: { x : Eigenwijs.Playground.Number, y : Eigenwijs.Playground.Number, down : Basics.Bool, click : Basics.Bool }

aliases.name: Screen
aliases.comment:
Get the dimensions of the screen. If the screen is 800 by 600, you will see
a value like this:

    { width = 800
    , height = 600
    , top = 300
    , left = -400
    , right = 400
    , bottom = -300
    }

This can be nice when used with [`moveY`](#moveY) if you want to put something
on the bottom of the screen, no matter the dimensions.
aliases.type: { width : Eigenwijs.Playground.Number, height : Eigenwijs.Playground.Number, top : Eigenwijs.Playground.Number, left : Eigenwijs.Playground.Number, right : Eigenwijs.Playground.Number, bottom : Eigenwijs.Playground.Number }

values.name: animation
values.comment:
Create an animation!

Once you get comfortable using [`picture`](#picture) to layout shapes, you can
try out an `animation`. Here is square that zigzags back and forth:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ square blue 40
            |> moveX (zigzag -100 100 2 time)
        ]

We need to define a `view` to make our animation work.

Within `view` we can use functions like [`spin`](#spin), [`wave`](#wave),
and [`zigzag`](#zigzag) to move and rotate our shapes.

values.name: animationInit
values.comment: Animation init

values.name: animationSubscriptions
values.comment: Animation subscriptions

values.name: animationUpdate
values.comment: Animation update function

values.name: animationView
values.comment: Animation view

values.name: beginOfTime
values.comment: BeginOfTime

values.name: circle
values.comment:
Make circles:

    dot =
        circle red 10

    sun =
        circle yellow 300

You give a color and then the radius. So the higher the number, the larger
the circle.

values.name: fade
values.comment:
Fade a shape. This lets you make shapes see-through or even completely
invisible. Here is a shape that fades in and out:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ square orange 30
        , square blue 200
            |> fade (zigzag 0 1 3 time)
        ]

The number has to be between `0` and `1`, where `0` is totally transparent
and `1` is completely solid.

values.name: game
values.comment:
Create a game!

Once you get comfortable with [`animation`](#animation), you can try making a
game with the keyboard and mouse. Here is an example of a green square that
just moves to the right:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update 0

    view computer offset =
        [ square green 40
            |> moveRight offset
        ]

    update computer offset =
        offset + 0.03

This shows the three important parts of a game:

1.  `memory` - makes it possible to store information. So with our green square,
    we save the `offset` in memory. It starts out at `0`.
2.  `view` - lets us say which shapes to put on screen. So here we move our
    square right by the `offset` saved in memory.
3.  `update` - lets us update the memory. We are incrementing the `offset` by
    a tiny amount on each frame.

The `update` function is called about 60 times per second, so our little
changes to `offset` start to add up pretty quickly!

This game is not very fun though! Making a `game` also gives you access to the
[`Computer`](#Computer), so you can use information about the [`Mouse`](#Mouse)
and [`Keyboard`](#Keyboard) to make it interactive! So here is a red square that
moves based on the arrow keys:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update ( 0, 0 )

    view computer ( x, y ) =
        [ square red 40
            |> move x y
        ]

    update computer ( x, y ) =
        ( x + toX computer.keyboard
        , y + toY computer.keyboard
        )

Notice that in the `update` we use information from the keyboard to update the
`x` and `y` values. These building blocks let you make pretty fancy games!
values.type: (Eigenwijs.Playground.Computer -> memory -> List.List Eigenwijs.Playground.Shape) -> (Eigenwijs.Playground.Computer -> memory -> memory) -> memory -> Platform.Program () (Eigenwijs.Playground.Game memory) Eigenwijs.Playground.Msg

values.name: gameInit
values.comment: Game init function

values.name: gameSubscriptions
values.comment: Game subscriptions

values.name: gameUpdate
values.comment: Game update function
values.type: (Eigenwijs.Playground.Computer -> memory -> memory) -> Eigenwijs.Playground.Msg -> Eigenwijs.Playground.Game memory -> Eigenwijs.Playground.Game memory

values.name: gameView
values.comment: Game view function
values.type: (Eigenwijs.Playground.Computer -> memory -> List.List Eigenwijs.Playground.Shape) -> Eigenwijs.Playground.Game memory -> Html.Html Eigenwijs.Playground.Msg

values.name: group
values.comment:
Put shapes together so you can [`move`](#move) and [`rotate`](#rotate)
them as a group. Maybe you want to put a bunch of stars in the sky:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ star
                |> move 100 100
                |> rotate 5
            , star
                |> move -120 40
                |> rotate 20
            , star
                |> move 80 -150
                |> rotate 32
            , star
                |> move -90 -30
                |> rotate -16
            ]

    star =
        group
            [ triangle yellow 20
            , triangle yellow 20
                |> rotate 180
            ]

values.name: hexagon
values.comment:
Make hexagons:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ hexagon lightYellow 50
            ]

The number is the radius, the distance from the center to each point.

If you made more hexagons, you could [`move`](#move) them around to make a
honeycomb pattern!

values.name: image
values.comment:
Add some image from the internet:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ image 96 96 "https://elm-lang.org/images/turtle.gif"
            ]

You provide the width, height, and then the URL of the image you want to show.

values.name: move
values.comment:
Move a shape by some number of pixels:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ square red 100
                |> move -60 60
            , square yellow 100
                |> move 60 60
            , square green 100
                |> move 60 -60
            , square blue 100
                |> move -60 -60
            ]

values.name: moveDown
values.comment:
Move a shape down by some number of pixels. So if you wanted to put the sky
above the ground, you could move the sky up and the ground down:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ rectangle lightBlue 200 100
                |> moveUp 50
            , rectangle lightGreen 200 100
                |> moveDown 50
            ]

values.name: moveLeft
values.comment:
Move shapes to the left.

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ circle yellow 10
                |> moveLeft 80
                |> moveUp 30
            ]

values.name: moveRight
values.comment:
Move shapes to the right.

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ square purple 20
                |> moveRight 80
                |> moveDown 100
            ]

values.name: moveUp
values.comment:
Move a shape up by some number of pixels. So if you wanted to make a tree
you could move the leaves up above the trunk:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ rectangle brown 40 200
            , circle green 100
                |> moveUp 180
            ]

values.name: moveX
values.comment:
Move the `x` coordinate of a shape by some amount. Here is a square that
moves back and forth:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ square purple 20
            |> moveX (wave 4 -200 200 time)
        ]

Using `moveX` feels a bit nicer here because the movement may be positive or negative.

values.name: moveY
values.comment:
Move the `y` coordinate of a shape by some amount. Maybe you want to make
grass along the bottom of the screen:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update 0

    update computer memory =
        memory

    view computer count =
        [ rectangle green computer.screen.width 100
            |> moveY computer.screen.bottom
        ]

Using `moveY` feels a bit nicer when setting things relative to the bottom or
top of the screen, since the values are negative sometimes.

values.name: octagon
values.comment:
Make octogons:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ octagon red 100
            ]

You give the color and radius, so each point of this stop sign is 100 pixels
from the center.

values.name: oval
values.comment:
Make ovals:

    football =
        oval brown 200 100

You give the color, and then the width and height. So our `football` example
is 200 pixels wide and 100 pixels tall.

values.name: pentagon
values.comment:
Make pentagons:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ pentagon darkGrey 100
            ]

You give the color and then the radius. So the distance from the center to each
of the five points is 100 pixels.

values.name: picture
values.comment:
Make a picture! Here is a picture of a triangle with an eyeball:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ triangle green 150
            , circle white 40
            , circle black 10
            ]

values.name: pictureInit
values.comment: Picture init function

values.name: pictureSubscriptions
values.comment: Picture subscriptions

values.name: pictureUpdate
values.comment: Picture update function

values.name: pictureView
values.comment: Picture view function

values.name: polygon
values.comment:
Make any shape you want! Here is a very thin triangle:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ polygon black [ ( -10, -20 ), ( 0, 100 ), ( 10, -20 ) ]
            ]

**Note:** If you [`rotate`](#rotate) a polygon, it will always rotate around
`(0,0)`. So it is best to build your shapes around that point, and then use
[`move`](#move) or [`group`](#group) so that rotation makes more sense.

values.name: rectangle
values.comment:
Make rectangles. This example makes a red cross:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ rectangle red 20 60
            , rectangle red 60 20
            ]

You give the color, width, and then height. So the first shape is vertical
part of the cross, the thinner and taller part.

values.name: rgb
values.comment:
RGB stands for Red-Green-Blue. With these three parts, you can create any
color you want. For example:

    brightBlue =
        rgb 18 147 216

    brightGreen =
        rgb 119 244 8

    brightPurple =
        rgb 94 28 221

Each number needs to be between 0 and 255.

It can be hard to figure out what numbers to pick, so try using a color picker
like [paletton] to find colors that look nice together. Once you find nice
colors, click on the color previews to get their RGB values.

[paletton]: http://paletton.com/

values.name: rotate
values.comment:
Rotate shapes in degrees.

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ words black "These words are tilted!"
                |> rotate 10
            ]

The degrees go **counter-clockwise** to match the direction of the
[unit circle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unit_circle).

values.name: scale
values.comment:
Make a shape bigger or smaller. So if you wanted some [`words`](#words) to
be larger, you could say:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ words black "Hello, nice to see you!"
                |> scale 3
            ]

values.name: spin
values.comment:
Create an angle that cycles from 0 to 360 degrees over time.

Here is an [`animation`](#animation) with a spinning triangle:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ triangle orange 50
            |> rotate (spin 8 time)
        ]

It will do a full rotation once every eight seconds. Try changing the `8` to
a `2` to make it do a full rotation every two seconds. It moves a lot faster!

values.name: square
values.comment:
Make squares. Here are two squares combined to look like an empty box:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ square purple 80
            , square white 60
            ]

The number you give is the dimension of each side. So that purple square would
be 80 pixels by 80 pixels.

values.name: toX
values.comment:
Turn the LEFT and RIGHT arrows into a number.

    toX { left = False, right = False, ... } == 0
    toX { left = True , right = False, ... } == -1
    toX { left = False, right = True , ... } == 1
    toX { left = True , right = True , ... } == 0

So to make a square move left and right based on the arrow keys, we could say:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update 0

    view computer x =
        [ square green 40
            |> moveX x
        ]

    update computer x =
        x + toX computer.keyboard

values.name: toXY
values.comment:
If you just use `toX` and `toY`, you will move diagonal too fast. You will go
right at 1 pixel per update, but you will go up/right at 1.41421 pixels per
update.

So `toXY` turns the arrow keys into an `(x,y)` pair such that the distance is
normalized:

    toXY { up = True , down = False, left = False, right = False, ... } == (1, 0)
    toXY { up = True , down = False, left = False, right = True , ... } == (0.707, 0.707)
    toXY { up = False, down = False, left = False, right = True , ... } == (0, 1)

Now when you go up/right, you are still going 1 pixel per update.

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update ( 0, 0 )

    view computer ( x, y ) =
        [ square green 40
            |> move x y
        ]

    update computer ( x, y ) =
        let
            ( dx, dy ) =
                toXY computer.keyboard
        in
        ( x + dx, y + dy )

values.name: toY
values.comment:
Turn the UP and DOWN arrows into a number.

    toY { up = False, down = False, ... } == 0
    toY { up = True , down = False, ... } == 1
    toY { up = False, down = True , ... } == -1
    toY { up = True , down = True , ... } == 0

This can be used to move characters around in games just like [`toX`](#toX):

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update ( 0, 0 )

    view computer ( x, y ) =
        [ square blue 40
            |> move x y
        ]

    update computer ( x, y ) =
        ( x + toX computer.keyboard
        , y + toY computer.keyboard
        )

values.name: triangle
values.comment:
Make triangles. So if you wanted to draw the Egyptian pyramids, you could
do a simple version like this:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ triangle darkYellow 200
            ]

The number is the "radius", so the distance from the center to each point of
the pyramid is `200`. Pretty big!

values.name: wave
values.comment:
Smoothly wave between two numbers.

Here is an [`animation`](#animation) with a circle that resizes:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ circle lightBlue (wave 50 90 7 time)
        ]

The radius of the circle will cycles between 50 and 90 every seven seconds.
It kind of looks like it is breathing.

values.name: words
values.comment:
Show some words!

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ words black "Hello! How are you?"
            ]

You can use [`scale`](#scale) to make the words bigger or smaller.

values.name: zigzag
values.comment:
Zig zag between two numbers.

Here is an [`animation`](#animation) with a rectangle that tips back and forth:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ rectangle lightGreen 20 100
            |> rotate (zigzag -20 20 4 time)
        ]

It gets rotated by an angle. The angle cycles from -20 degrees to 20 degrees
every four seconds.

name: Eigenwijs.Playground3d

Shapes help you make a `picture`, `animation`, or `game`.

Read on to see examples of [`circle`](#circle), [`rectangle`](#rectangle),
[`words`](#words), [`image`](#image), and many more!

unions.name: Time
unions.comment:
The current time.

Helpful when making an [`animation`](#animation) with functions like
[`spin`](#spin), [`wave`](#wave), and [`zigzag`](#zigzag).

aliases.comment:
When writing a [`game`](#game), you can look up all sorts of information
about your computer:

  - [`Mouse`](#Mouse) - Where is the mouse right now?
  - [`Keyboard`](#Keyboard) - Are the arrow keys down?
  - [`Screen`](#Screen) - How wide is the screen?
  - [`Time`](#Time) - What time is it right now?

So you can use expressions like `computer.mouse.x` and `computer.keyboard.enter`
in games where you want some mouse or keyboard interaction.
aliases.type: { inbox : List.List Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Message, secondsBeforeSend : Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Number, touch : { list : List.List Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Touch, current : Maybe.Maybe Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Touch, change : Maybe.Maybe Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Touch, previous : Maybe.Maybe Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Touch }, mouse : Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Mouse, keyboard : Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Keyboard, screen : Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Screen, time : Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Time }

aliases.name: Connection
aliases.comment:
A network Connection with server url, sender name, and the send interval
in seconds.
aliases.type: { server : String.String, name : String.String, sendIntervalInSeconds : Basics.Int }

Figure out what is going on with the mouse.

You could draw a circle around the mouse with a program like this:

    import Playground exposing (..)

        [ circle yellow 40
            |> moveX computer.mouse.x
            |> moveY computer.mouse.y
        ]

    update computer memory =
        memory

You could also use `computer.mouse.down` to change the color of the circle
while the mouse button is down.

Get the dimensions of the screen. If the screen is 800 by 600, you will see

    { width = 800
    , height = 600
    , top = 300
    , left = -400
    , right = 400
    , bottom = -300
    }

This can be nice when used with [`moveY`](#moveY) if you want to put something
on the bottom of the screen, no matter the dimensions.

values.name: animation
values.comment:
Create an animation!

Once you get comfortable using [`picture`](#picture) to layout shapes, you can
try out an `animation`. Here is square that zigzags back and forth:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ square blue 40
            |> moveX (zigzag -100 100 2 time)
        ]

We need to define a `view` to make our animation work.

Within `view` we can use functions like [`spin`](#spin), [`wave`](#wave),
and [`zigzag`](#zigzag) to move and rotate our shapes.

values.name: animationInit
values.comment: Animation init

values.name: animationSubscriptions
values.comment: Animation subscriptions

values.name: animationUpdate
values.comment: Animation update function

values.name: animationView
values.comment: Animation view

values.name: beginOfTime
values.comment: BeginOfTime

values.name: block
values.comment:
Make blocks. This example makes a red cross:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ block red 20 60 10
            , block red 60 20 10
            ]

You give the color, width, height, and then depth. So the first shape is the
vertical part of the cross, the thinner and taller part.

values.name: circle
values.comment:
Make circles:

    dot =
        circle red 10

    sun =
        circle yellow 300

You give a color and then the radius. So the higher the number, the larger
the circle.

values.name: cube
values.comment:
Make cubes:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ cube blue 10
            ]

You give the color, width, height, and then depth. So the first shape is the
vertical part of the cross, the thinner and taller part.

values.name: cylinder
values.comment:
Make cylinders:

    pillar =
        cylinder red 10 50

You give a color, the radius and then the height.

values.name: entity
values.comment: Add a 3D shape to a Scene3D scene as an entity.

values.name: extrude
values.comment:
Pull a 2D shape "up" to form a 3D body:

    circle blue 20
        |> extrude 50

This will create a flat circle "extruded" along the
z-axis to form a 3D cylinder. The extruded form is
centered around the xy-plane.

values.name: eyesAt
values.comment: Create a camera looking from a point x y z, towards the origin (0, 0, 0)

values.name: fade
values.comment:
Fade a shape. This lets you make shapes see-through or even completely
invisible. Here is a shape that fades in and out:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ square orange 30
        , square blue 200
            |> fade (zigzag 0 1 3 time)
        ]

The number has to be between `0` and `1`, where `0` is totally transparent
and `1` is completely solid.

values.name: game
values.comment:
Create a game!

Once you get comfortable with [`animation`](#animation), you can try making a
game with the keyboard and mouse. Here is an example of a green square that
just moves to the right:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update 0

    view computer offset =
        [ square green 40
            |> moveRight offset
        ]

    update computer offset =
        offset + 0.03

This shows the three important parts of a game:

1.  `memory` - makes it possible to store information. So with our green square,
    we save the `offset` in memory. It starts out at `0`.
2.  `view` - lets us say which shapes to put on screen. So here we move our
    square right by the `offset` saved in memory.
3.  `update` - lets us update the memory. We are incrementing the `offset` by
    a tiny amount on each frame.

The `update` function is called about 60 times per second, so our little
changes to `offset` start to add up pretty quickly!

This game is not very fun though! Making a `game` also gives you access to the
[`Computer`](#Computer), so you can use information about the [`Mouse`](#Mouse)
and [`Keyboard`](#Keyboard) to make it interactive! So here is a red square that
moves based on the arrow keys:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update ( 0, 0 )

    view computer ( x, y ) =
        [ square red 40
            |> move x y
        ]

    update computer ( x, y ) =
        ( x + toX computer.keyboard
        , y + toY computer.keyboard
        )

Notice that in the `update` we use information from the keyboard to update the
`x` and `y` values. These building blocks let you make pretty fancy games!
values.type: (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Computer -> memory -> List.List Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Shape) -> (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Computer -> memory -> memory) -> memory -> Platform.Program () (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Game memory) Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Msg

values.name: gameInit
values.comment: Game init function

values.name: gameSubscriptions
values.comment: Game subscriptions

values.name: gameUpdate
values.comment: Game update function
values.type: (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Computer -> memory -> memory) -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Msg -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Game memory -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Game memory

values.name: gameView
values.comment: Game view function
values.type: (memory -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Camera) -> (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Computer -> memory -> List.List Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Shape) -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Game memory -> Html.Html Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Msg

values.name: gameWithCamera
values.comment: Create a game using a specific camera for viewing the scene!
values.type: (memory -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Camera) -> (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Computer -> memory -> List.List Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Shape) -> (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Computer -> memory -> memory) -> memory -> Platform.Program () (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Game memory) Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Msg

values.name: group
values.comment:
Put shapes together so you can [`move`](#move) and [`rotate`](#rotate)
them as a group. Maybe you want to put a bunch of stars in the sky:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ star
                |> move 100 100
                |> rotate 5
            , star
                |> move -120 40
                |> rotate 20
            , star
                |> move 80 -150
                |> rotate 32
            , star
                |> move -90 -30
                |> rotate -16
            ]

    star =
        group
            [ triangle yellow 20
            , triangle yellow 20
                |> rotate 180
            ]

values.name: isometric
values.comment: Create an isometric camera

values.name: lookAt
values.comment: Modify a camera to look at a specific point x y z

values.name: move
values.comment:
Move a shape by some number of pixels:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ square red 100
                |> move -60 60 0
            , square yellow 100
                |> move 60 60 0
            , square green 100
                |> move 60 -60 0
            , square blue 100
                |> move -60 -60 0
            ]

values.name: moveX
values.comment:
Move the `x` coordinate of a shape by some amount. Here is a square that
moves back and forth:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ square purple 20
            |> moveX (wave 4 -200 200 time)
        ]

Using `moveX` feels a bit nicer here because the movement may be positive or negative.

values.name: moveY
values.comment:
Move the `y` coordinate of a shape by some amount. Maybe you want to make
grass along the bottom of the screen:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update 0

    update computer memory =
        memory

    view computer count =
        [ rectangle green computer.screen.width 100
            |> moveY computer.screen.bottom
        ]

Using `moveY` feels a bit nicer when setting things relative to the bottom or
top of the screen, since the values are negative sometimes.

values.name: moveZ
values.comment: Move the `z` coordinate of a shape by some amount:

values.name: networkGame
values.comment:
Create a network game; a game that has a messages `inbox` field in Computer
and a messages `outbox` as a field in its memory. The game takes a Connection
parameter. Messages sent are tagged with your sender name, configured as
`name` in the Connection.
values.type: Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Connection -> (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Computer -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.WithMessages memory -> List.List Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Shape) -> (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Computer -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.WithMessages memory -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.WithMessages memory) -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.WithMessages memory -> Platform.Program () (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Game (Eigenwijs.Playground3d.WithMessages memory)) Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Msg

values.name: picture
values.comment:
Make a picture! Here is a picture of a triangle with an eyeball:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ triangle green 150
            , circle white 40
            , circle black 10
            ]

values.name: pictureInit
values.comment: Picture init function

values.name: pictureSubscriptions
values.comment: Picture subscriptions

values.name: pictureUpdate
values.comment: Picture update function

values.name: pictureView
values.comment: Picture view function

values.name: pitch
values.comment:
Rotate shapes in degrees, along the Y axis:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ triangle black 50
                |> pitch 10
            ]

    Search Wikipedia for "roll, pitch, yaw" to find out why it is called "pitch". ;)

values.name: polygon
values.comment:
Make any shape you want! Here is a very thin triangle:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ polygon black [ ( -10, -20 ), ( 0, 100 ), ( 10, -20 ) ]
            ]

**Note:** If you [`rotate`](#rotate) a polygon, it will always rotate around
`(0,0)`. So it is best to build your shapes around that point, and then use
[`move`](#move) or [`group`](#group) so that rotation makes more sense.

values.name: pullUp
values.comment:
Pull a 2D shape "up" to form a 3D body:

    circle blue 20
        |> pullUp 50

This will create a flat circle "pulled up" (or "extruded") along the
z-axis to form a 3D cylinder.

values.name: rectangle
values.comment:
Make rectangles. This example makes a red cross:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ rectangle red 20 60
            , rectangle red 60 20
            ]

You give the color, width, and then height. So the first shape is vertical
part of the cross, the thinner and taller part.

values.name: rgb
values.comment:
RGB stands for Red-Green-Blue. With these three parts, you can create any
color you want. For example:

    brightBlue =
        rgb 0.1 0.6 1

    brightGreen =
        rgb 0.6 1 0.05

    brightPurple =
        rgb 0.4 0.1 0.8

For `rgb` each numer is expected to be a floating point number between 0 and 1.
Also see `rgb255`, where each number needs to be an integer between 0 and 255.

It can be hard to figure out what numbers to pick, so try using a color picker
like [paletton] to find colors that look nice together. Once you find nice
colors, click on the color previews to get their RGB values.

[paletton]: http://paletton.com/

values.name: rgb255
values.comment:
RGB stands for Red-Green-Blue. With these three parts, you can create any
color you want. For example:

    brightBlue =
        rgb255 18 147 216

    brightGreen =
        rgb255 119 244 8

    brightPurple =
        rgb255 94 28 221

For `rgb255` each number needs to be an integer between 0 and 255.
Also see `rgb`, where each numer is expected to be a floating point number between 0 and 1.

It can be hard to figure out what numbers to pick, so try using a color picker
like [paletton] to find colors that look nice together. Once you find nice
colors, click on the color previews to get their RGB values.

[paletton]: http://paletton.com/

values.name: roll
values.comment:
Rotate shapes in degrees, along the X axis:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ triangle black 50
                |> roll 10
            ]

    Search Wikipedia for "roll, pitch, yaw" to find out why it is called "roll". ;)

values.name: rotate
values.comment:
Rotate shapes in degrees.

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ triangle black 50
                |> rotate 10
            ]

The degrees go **counter-clockwise** to match the direction of the
[unit circle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unit_circle).

values.name: scale
values.comment:
Make a shape bigger or smaller. So if you wanted some [`words`](#words) to
be larger, you could say:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ words black "Hello, nice to see you!"
                |> scale 3
            ]

values.name: secondsBetween
values.comment: Return the number of seconds between two instances of Time.

values.name: snake
values.comment: Make a snake!
values.type: Color.Color -> List.List ( Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Number, Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Number, Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Number ) -> Eigenwijs.Playground3d.Shape

values.name: sphere
values.comment:
Make sphere:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ sphere green 10
            ]

You give the color, width, height, and then depth. So the first shape is the
vertical part of the cross, the thinner and taller part.

values.name: spin
values.comment:
Create an angle that cycles from 0 to 360 degrees over time.

Here is an [`animation`](#animation) with a spinning triangle:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ triangle orange 50
            |> rotate (spin 8 time)
        ]

It will do a full rotation once every eight seconds. Try changing the `8` to
a `2` to make it do a full rotation every two seconds. It moves a lot faster!

values.name: square
values.comment:
Make squares. Here are two squares combined to look like an empty box:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ square purple 80
            , square white 60
            ]

The number you give is the dimension of each side. So that purple square would
be 80 pixels by 80 pixels.

values.name: toX
values.comment:
Turn the LEFT and RIGHT arrows into a number.

    toX { left = False, right = False, ... } == 0
    toX { left = True , right = False, ... } == -1
    toX { left = False, right = True , ... } == 1
    toX { left = True , right = True , ... } == 0

So to make a square move left and right based on the arrow keys, we could say:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update 0

    view computer x =
        [ square green 40
            |> moveX x
        ]

    update computer x =
        x + toX computer.keyboard

values.name: toXY
values.comment:
If you just use `toX` and `toY`, you will move diagonal too fast. You will go
right at 1 pixel per update, but you will go up/right at 1.41421 pixels per
update.

So `toXY` turns the arrow keys into an `(x,y)` pair such that the distance is
normalized:

    toXY { up = True , down = False, left = False, right = False, ... } == (1, 0)
    toXY { up = True , down = False, left = False, right = True , ... } == (0.707, 0.707)
    toXY { up = False, down = False, left = False, right = True , ... } == (0, 1)

Now when you go up/right, you are still going 1 pixel per update.

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update ( 0, 0 )

    view computer ( x, y ) =
        [ square green 40
            |> move x y
        ]

    update computer ( x, y ) =
        let
            ( dx, dy ) =
                toXY computer.keyboard
        in
        ( x + dx, y + dy )

values.name: toY
values.comment:
Turn the UP and DOWN arrows into a number.

    toY { up = False, down = False, ... } == 0
    toY { up = True , down = False, ... } == 1
    toY { up = False, down = True , ... } == -1
    toY { up = True , down = True , ... } == 0

This can be used to move characters around in games just like [`toX`](#toX):

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        game view update ( 0, 0 )

    view computer ( x, y ) =
        [ square blue 40
            |> move x y
        ]

    update computer ( x, y ) =
        ( x + toX computer.keyboard
        , y + toY computer.keyboard
        )

values.name: triangle
values.comment:
Make triangles:

    triangle blue 50

You give a color and then its size.

values.name: wave
values.comment:
Smoothly wave between two numbers.

Here is an [`animation`](#animation) with a circle that resizes:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ circle lightBlue (wave 50 90 7 time)
        ]

The radius of the circle will cycles between 50 and 90 every seven seconds.
It kind of looks like it is breathing.

values.name: yaw
values.comment:
Rotate shapes in degrees, along the Z axis:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        picture
            [ triangle black 50
                |> yaw 10
            ]

    Search Wikipedia for "roll, pitch, yaw" to find out why it is called "yaw". ;)

values.name: zigzag
values.comment:
Zig zag between two numbers.

Here is an [`animation`](#animation) with a rectangle that tips back and forth:

    import Playground exposing (..)

    main =
        animation view

    view time =
        [ rectangle lightGreen 20 100
            |> rotate (zigzag -20 20 4 time)
        ]

It gets rotated by an angle. The angle cycles from -20 degrees to 20 degrees
every four seconds.
